original_abstract:
This paper explores the application of deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for the analysis and classification of remote sensing data. While deep learning has revolutionized computer vision, its direct application to multi- and hyperspectral imagery is often hindered by high-dimensional input spaces and the scarcity of expert-labeled training samples. To address these challenges, we propose an efficient framework based on greedy layer-wise unsupervised pre-training. 

Central to our approach is the Enforcing Lifetime and Population Sparsity (EPLS) algorithm, a computationally efficient method for unsupervised feature learning that simultaneously promotes both forms of sparsity without requiring extensive meta-parameter tuning. We demonstrate the versatility and discriminative power of the resulting hierarchical representations across four diverse remote sensing tasks: aerial scene classification, land-use mapping in very high resolution (VHR) imagery, and land-cover classification using multispectral and hyperspectral data. 

Our experimental results indicate that the proposed method significantly outperforms traditional dimensionality reduction techniques, such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and its kernelized version (kPCA), as well as existing state-of-the-art algorithms for aerial imagery. We find that while single-layer networks can extract useful features—provided they incorporate local spatial context—deep architectures offer superior performance by capturing increasingly abstract and complex features. This research suggests that unsupervised deep feature extraction is a robust and scalable solution for the challenges of modern Earth observation data analysis.
humanized_abstract:
Deep learning based approach has gained significant attention in the field of computer vision. Analysis and classification of remote sensing data using deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) is explored in this paper. Deep learning is a sub field of machine learning which has made tremendous progress in the field of computer vision. But in the case of multi- and hyperspectral remote sensing data, applying deep learning is a challenging task due to the high dimensional input space and the scarcity of expert labeled training samples. In this paper, we propose an efficient approach using greedy layer-wise unsupervised pre-training.

As core component, we propose an efficient unsupervised learning algorithm called Enforcing Lifetime and Population Sparsity (EPLS) which can learn features in a computationally efficient manner and encourages both types of sparse features without requiring any parametric adjustment. The discriminative capacity of the hierarchical features learned with EPLS is then validated extensively through four representative remote sensing applications, including aerial scene classification, land use classification from VHR images and land cover classification from multispectral and hyperspectral data.

In this work, we compared the performance of state-of-the-art methods for feature learning from Earth observation data with our proposed method and demonstrated that it greatly outperforms common dimensionality reduction techniques, such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and its kernel extension kPCA, as well as other relevant approaches for remote sensing. The benefits of using deep features are due to the fact that shallower models, even when including a spatial feature learning layer, often use only low-level features, which are obtained from the raw data and tend to be local and include little discriminative information. In contrast, deep models are able to learn a rich set of features with increasingly abstract and high-level information contents. The unsupervised deep feature learning therefore appears to be an efficient approach for analyzing large-scale EO data, which is required for many contemporary Earth observation applications.

This paper explores the application of deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for the analysis and classification of remote sensing data. While deep learning has revolutionized computer vision, its direct application to multi- and hyperspectral imagery is often hindered by high-dimensional input spaces and the scarcity of expert-labeled training samples.
